QF.id: XIII Introduction to the Underlying Markets_16

question.text: What distinguishes equity securities from debt securities as investment instruments?

answer.text: Equity represents ownership in a company with variable returns; debt represents a loan to the issuer with fixed or determinable returns
Equity always pays regular dividends; debt never pays any periodic income
Equity has a fixed maturity date; debt has no maturity and is perpetual
Equity is only traded on exchanges; debt is only traded over-the-counter

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Equity represents ownership (residual claim on assets and earnings) with variable returns (dividends + capital appreciation). Debt represents a creditor relationship with fixed/determinable returns (coupon payments) and a maturity date. **The Trap:** Equity dividends are not guaranteed (companies may skip them). Some debt instruments are perpetual. Both equity and debt can trade on exchanges and OTC. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV Ch1 compares equity vs debt. Series VIII covers equity derivatives. Series XII covers both asset classes at a foundational level. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** In derivative markets, the underlying asset class determines the pricing model — equity derivatives use cost-of-carry, while IR derivatives use bond math.
